title: Hypothermia in newborn lambs
subtitle: Recognising it, warming the lamb, and when to tube-feed.
excerpt: A wet lamb born into wind and rain can be hypothermic within 30 minutes of birth. The temperature of the lamb and its age since birth determine the treatment. Warming a lamb that still has viable energy reserves is straightforward; a lamb that has burned through its reserves needs colostrum by tube before warmth will save it.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Sheep Welfare Code. SAC Consulting Scotland, Lambing Guide. AHDB Beef and Lamb, Lamb Survival Guide. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, lambing chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The thick, yellowish first milk produced by a ewe in the hours after lambing; rich in antibodies and energy, essential for lamb survival in the first 24 hours.
Passing a soft rubber tube down the lamb's throat into the stomach and delivering liquid directly; used when the lamb is too weak to suck from the ewe.

content.text: Hypothermia in a newborn lamb is the most common cause of lamb death in the first three days of life in the UK. It sets in when the lamb loses heat faster than it can generate it from its brown-fat reserves, the reserves are enough for about two hours in cold, wet, windy conditions. After that, the lamb's core temperature falls and it becomes unresponsive. Treatment depends on whether the lamb is less than five hours old or more, because the management differs significantly.

A hypothermic lamb is cold to the touch at the mouth and in the armpits. It may be unable to stand, limp, slow to respond to handling, or unable to suck. A lamb that is warm to touch but limp and unresponsive is probably depleted of energy (hypoglycaemic) rather than hypothermic, the treatment overlaps but is not identical.

A lamb under five hours old that is cold but still has a swallow reflex can be warmed directly. Dry it, put it in a warming box at around 38°C, or tuck it against your body under your coat. Once it is warm and moving, make sure it has suckled from the ewe or been

A lamb more than five hours old that is hypothermic has likely exhausted its brown-fat reserves. Warming it before tube-feeding is counterproductive, the metabolic demand of rewarming depletes what little glucose remains. For these lambs: tube-feed first (50 ml per kg of colostrum or colostrum substitute) and then begin warming. Give the glucose time to reach the blood before the muscles start demanding fuel.
Measure the stomach tube from the lamb's mouth to its last rib and mark the tube with tape. This length is the depth to insert.

Hold the lamb sitting up, head slightly extended. Slide the tube over the tongue and down the oesophagus. The tube passes easily into the stomach; resistance means you're in the trachea, withdraw and retry.
Attach the syringe to the end of the tube. Deliver colostrum slowly, 50 ml per kg bodyweight is the safe dose for a first feed.
Withdraw the tube slowly. Kink it just before the last few centimetres come out so the remaining liquid doesn't drip into the airway as you remove it.

Return the warmed, fed lamb to the ewe in a small pen where she can't move away. Smear the lamb with birth fluids from the ewe if possible, or hold the ewe and introduce the lamb to her teat directly. A ewe that was separated from her lamb during the warming period may reject it; watch for 30 minutes and assist if she turns away.
